image:image.image:title: 8 Years Building the Global Leading Gamification
image:image.image:caption: 8 Years Building the Global Leading Gamification : Teams use visible mechanics, but the design still fails when it does not protect motivation, identity, and repeat behavior in real work.

image:image.image:title: Building Beats Playing
image:image.image:caption: Building Beats Playing: Matt Dalio’s Path: Teams care about the topic, but the surrounding systems still produce activity without durable capability, better decisions, or visible business impact.

image:image.image:title: Effort isn't the problem. Your learning system is
image:image.image:caption: Most learning failures are system failures: passive methods, weak feedback, and no spaced retrieval.

image:image.image:title: Design thinking is not retention
image:image.image:caption: Design thinking improves usability. Retention needs a behaviour model that makes people return.

image:image.image:title: Learning games fail when we try to teach everything
image:image.image:caption: Learning games break when they try to teach everything. Design around one high value behaviour, then scale.

image:image.image:title: Delight belongs in the core loop
image:image.image:caption: Delight is not garnish. Research on user experience shows that appeal comes from the interplay of pragmatic and hedonic quality during use, not from decorative extras added at the end.

image:image.image:title: Novelty is not retention
image:image.image:caption: Gamification can help people start, but it rarely keeps them by itself. Longitudinal research shows that once novelty fades, retention depends on meaningful progress, tailored design, and autonomy.

image:image.image:title: Why teams reject AI that feels like replacement
image:image.image:caption: AI adoption usually fails for a human reason. When the system feels like control or replacement, teams resist it. When it supports autonomy, competence, and visible wins, usage rises.

image:image.image:title: When a leaderboard becomes surveillance
image:image.image:caption: Leaderboards can create short-term movement, but in workplace learning they often drift into surveillance. The better design question is whether the system strengthens autonomy, competence, and useful feedback.
